title: TwinBench: An Auditable Cluster Benchmark for Coupled Invariance and Boundary Sensitivity in QA
description: TwinBench is a benchmark-engineering paper rather than a novelty-first benchmark paper. Its atomic release unit is a verified QA cluster with answer-preserving paraphrases and one minimal answer-changing flip, scored jointly with deterministic gold recomputation and an explicit audit protocol.
motivation: RoParQ, CounterBench, MQuAKE, LastingBench, and LGIP already show that paraphrase robustness, counterfactual answer changes, edited evidence, and coupled invariance/sensitivity matter. The gap is a small, auditable QA benchmark whose release object couples these behaviors on the same seed item and is strong on evaluation quality: deterministic recomputation, annotation rubrics, rejection logs, and split-wise construction analysis.
proposed_approach: Construct a 72-cluster procedural core plus an optional 24-cluster evidence-grounded extension. Each released cluster contains q0-q3 as the semantic core and q4 as a separate format control; all q3 labels must be deterministically recomputable, all items must pass dual human verification and adjudication, and the evidence-grounded slice enters the core release only if it passes pre-registered uniqueness and agreement gates.

related_work.title: RoParQ: Paraphrase-Aware Alignment of Large Language Models Towards Robustness to Paraphrased Questions
related_work.how_it_differs: RoParQ is the closest QA paraphrase benchmark, but it studies paraphrase robustness as a standalone setting and is not centered on a verified cluster release unit with coupled scoring and deterministic flip recomputation.

related_work.title: CounterBench: A Benchmark for Counterfactuals Reasoning in Large Language Models
related_work.authors: Yuefei Chen, Vivek K. Singh, Jing Ma, Ruixiang Tang
related_work.how_it_differs: CounterBench evaluates answer-changing counterfactual reasoning, but it does not tie those flips to answer-preserving paraphrases of the same seed item or release an auditable cluster as the primary unit.

related_work.title: MQuAKE: Assessing Knowledge Editing in Language Models via Multi-Hop Questions
related_work.authors: Zexuan Zhong, Zhengxuan Wu, Christopher D. Manning, Christopher Potts, Danqi Chen
related_work.how_it_differs: MQuAKE is the closest answer-updating neighbor, but it is aimed at knowledge-editing evaluation. TwinBench instead evaluates raw models on tightly localized, release-time verified clusters with deterministic gold answers.

related_work.title: LastingBench: Defend Benchmarks Against Knowledge Leakage
related_work.authors: Yixiong Fang, Tianran Sun, Yuling Shi, Min Wang, Xiaodong Gu
related_work.how_it_differs: LastingBench already uses counterfactual rewriting, so TwinBench does not claim novelty for edited evidence alone. The difference is objective: small auditable QA clusters and coupled invariance/sensitivity scoring rather than long-context benchmark defense.

related_work.title: Language-Guided Invariance Probing of Vision-Language Models
related_work.how_it_differs: LGIP combines invariance to paraphrases and sensitivity to semantic flips in an adjacent vision-language domain. TwinBench adopts a similar high-level evaluation perspective but contributes a QA-specific benchmark with deterministic answer recomputation and a human audit protocol.

related_work.title: Do Large Language Model Benchmarks Test Reliability?
related_work.authors: Joshua Vendrow, Edward Vendrow, Sara Beery, Aleksander Madry
related_work.how_it_differs: This work motivates platinum-style curation. TwinBench instantiates that benchmark-quality philosophy for coupled semantic clusters rather than for benchmark cleaning alone.

related_work.title: BetterBench: Assessing AI Benchmarks, Uncovering Issues, and Establishing Best Practices
related_work.authors: Anka Reuel, Amelia Hardy, Chandler Smith, Max Lamparth, Malcolm Hardy, Mykel J. Kochenderfer
related_work.how_it_differs: BetterBench provides benchmark-quality principles. TwinBench turns those principles into a concrete benchmark object with acceptance gates, rubric-driven annotation, and release metadata.

hypothesis: Open instruction-tuned LLMs will show materially lower performance on coupled cluster semantics than on canonical seed accuracy, and the benchmark’s main value will be in exposing repeatable failure patterns and benchmark-construction brittleness rather than in supporting strong leaderboard claims.
success_criteria: Average Cluster Semantic Score is at least 8 percentage points below q0 seed accuracy across the four main local models.
Cluster Semantic Score is at least 5 percentage points below update-only accuracy on at least two task families, showing that coupling invariance with answer updating adds diagnostic signal.

Dual-annotation agreement reaches at least 0.80 on paraphrase validity and flip locality, and at least 0.85 on answer uniqueness and answer-change validity for any evidence-grounded items admitted to the core release.
The benchmark release includes an auditable construction table covering candidate-to-kept rates, rejection reasons, adjudication rates, and split-wise consistency.
Any rank differences between q0 accuracy and Cluster Semantic Score are reported only as exploratory unless bootstrap uncertainty clearly separates them.
